method2.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The Japanese name Nishinoshima is glossed as “western island”.[2] The island was first recorded in 1702 by the Spanish ship Rosario and was initially named Rosario Island, before later being renamed Nishinoshima.[3]

==Geology==
Nishinoshima is an active andesitic volcanic island at the summit of a large submarine volcano about 30 km in diameter.[1] It forms part of the volcanic front of the Izu–Bonin arc, along the Shichito-Iwojima Ridge, which trends roughly north–south parallel to the Ogasawara Trench.[4] Volcanism in the region is related to subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Philippine Sea Plate.[4] The volcano consists of the emergent main peak, two submarine peaks to the west and south, and a small cone on the northeast flank that may be a satellite cone.[4]

Before the recent enlargement of the island, Nishinoshima was described as about 650 m long from southwest to northeast, about 200 m wide, with an area of approximately 77,000 m2 and an elevation of 25 m.[1] Several reefs occur on the northeast side of the island.[1] The first recorded eruption began in May 1973 on the sea floor about 400 m east of the southern tip of the island, and by September 1973 it had formed a new island.[1] That eruption began as an undersea phreatomagmatic explosion, migrated in a semicircular pattern, and produced five craters that emitted volcanic blocks and lava flows.[1] Although wave erosion removed much of the new volcanic edifices, reworked deposits eventually linked the old and new islands, and the mouth of the intervening bay was closed by deposit movement in 1982.[1] Ejecta from the 1973–1974 activity had SiO2 contents of 51.8–60.1 wt%.[1]

A new eruptive phase began in 2013, producing a new island that merged with the older Nishinoshima by late 2013.[2] The 2013–2020 activity has been divided into four eruptive episodes, with the first lasting about two years, the second in 2017 lasting about 120 days, and the fourth in 2019–2020 lasting about 270 days.[3] Average effusion rates have been estimated at 2.0 × 105 m3/day for the 2013–2015 eruption and 1.5 × 105 m3/day for the 2017 eruption.[3] Observations of these eruptions relied heavily on satellites, aircraft, and research vessels, including optical imagery and synthetic-aperture radar data capable of tracking surface changes despite clouds or eruption plumes.[4]

The 2020 eruption included basalt, basaltic andesite, andesite, and dacite magmas in submarine deposits.[3] The andesites resemble those erupted from Nishinoshima since 1973, while the 2020 basalts are comparable to older basalts known from surrounding submarine knolls.[3] This suggests that basaltic magmas may have been generated throughout Nishinoshima’s history, with the eruption center shifting to the island summit in 2020.[3] Nishinoshima andesitic primary magmas are interpreted to originate directly from the mantle through shallow, hydrous melting of plagioclase peridotite.[3]

The 2019–2020 episode showed a notable change in eruptive style, beginning with Strombolian activity and lava effusion, progressing to lava-fountain eruptions, and ending with ash emission probably generated by phreatomagmatic activity.[4] This production of fine ash by a mechanism involving magma–water interaction had not been observed in previous Nishinoshima eruptions.[4]

==Ecology==
Nishinoshima is an isolated oceanic island in the Ogasawara chain, about 130 km west of Chichijima, and its remoteness makes it part of an archipelago noted for distinctive island ecosystems.[4][5]  
The Ogasawara Islands support many endemic and rare species, reflecting adaptive radiation and speciation on isolated oceanic islands.[5]  
Ecological conditions vary markedly among the islands because of differences in climate, topography and the diverse geographic origins of colonizing biota.[5]  
On Nishinoshima, volcanic eruptions and marine erosion repeatedly reshape the land surface, creating unstable habitats that can reset ecological succession.[5]  
The island is therefore important as a real-time case study of colonization and ecosystem development on newly formed volcanic terrain.[3][4]  
Plant colonization of the new volcanic areas of Nishinoshima has been studied since the post-1973 eruption period, including early research on the invasion of plants on Nishino-shima-shinto.[4]  
Frequent eruptions can nearly eliminate terrestrial vegetation, and a 2019 eruption was reported to have caused the local extinction of purslane, Portulaca oleracea, on the island despite the species occurring widely elsewhere.[2]  
Researchers collected some purslane plants before that eruption, illustrating the conservation value of monitoring short-lived biological communities on the island.[2]  
Ongoing scientific monitoring also examines species colonization, ecosystem resilience and the development of marine communities such as coral reefs around the expanding volcanic landmass.[3]
